headline: 240-Year-Old Pittsburgh Paper Finds New Life Under Nonprofit
meta_description: The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, America's first newspaper west of the Allegheny Mountains, has been rescued from bankruptcy by a nonprofit dedicated to local jour…
summary_short: After 20 years of losses, the historic Pittsburgh Post-Gazette has found salvation through the Venetoulis Institute, which believes local journalism is essential to stro…
body:
The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette will continue telling Pittsburgh's stories. On May 4th, the nonprofit Venetoulis Institute for Local Journalism acquired the storied publication, rescuing it from the financial collapse that has claimed so many American newspapers.

The paper's resilience matters. Founded 240 years ago—the same year the Constitution was drafted—the Post-Gazette published one of its first editions featuring the text of that revolutionary document. It became the first American newspaper west of the Allegheny Mountains, a distinction it has held ever since.

But survival had become precarious. Operating at a loss for two decades and hemorrhaging roughly $350 million, the paper seemed destined for the history books rather than the newsstand. The Institute, owned by hotel magnate Stewart Bainum Jr., acquired the Baltimore Banner in 2022 and now brings that same rescue mission to Pittsburgh.

The Post-Gazette's journalism credentials run deep. It has won three Pulitzer Prizes, including one in 1938 for exposing a Supreme Court justice's Ku Klux Klan membership, and another in 2019 for coverage of the Pittsburgh synagogue shooting.

While restructuring lies ahead—all 171 writers must reapply—limited print editions will continue on Thursdays and Sundays. For Pittsburgh, that's worth celebrating.
